sentences.text: We were talking about market days and the violence that quite often occurred on market days in form of a pogrom.
I recall such days.
Of course, the object was to get out of their way, to hide, until the drunken farmers would leave town and life would begin to be normal again.
Unfortunately, quite often, those violent outbursts were led by the priest, or clergy, who, by and large, were uneducated drunkards.
And occasionally, on Polish holidays, they would have processions where they would march with their icons through the streets.
And they were of the belief that any Jew who shows up in town, or who walks in the same street, violates their holiday.
And that would be occasion for a pogrom, occasionally.
Also, around Passover, there was always the fear of those horrible, wild, unfounded accusations.
It was called the blood [INAUDIBLE] about the-- the legend that Jewish people use Christian children's blood for baking matzah.
And that was, quite often, the occasion for a pogrom.
No, not I, personally, but my town, yes, there were such accusations.
Yes, yes, I did.
Yes, I did.
Yeah.
Not from pogroms that I can recall, although I know that there were fights.

By the time, in the 1930s, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] socialism came into town and workers were organized, not based on religious affiliation but rather, on the working class-- class versus the capitalist class, then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews began to arm themselves with weapons, and to protect themselves against such pogroms.
But that was just in the late 1930s, soon before the war began.
Yes.
I very clearly remember my oldest sisters-- two of my sister's married two brothers, by the name of Bailach, Chaim and Avremel Bailach.
And they were both members of the socialist movement.
And I remember when they used to come to visit the house, even before they married my sisters, they used to bring strange weapons that fascinated me as a child.
For instance, one of them was steel knuckles that you would put on you hand.
And if you were to hit somebody, you hit them with steel.
Another one was springs-- heavy springs that had a ball of iron of iron at the end of it that you would swing if a fight broke out.
I remember they used to hide them under a bed when they would come into the house.
And occasionally, I would sneak under and to-- to see what-- what they were.
But yes, we-- things began to change drastically in the 1930s.
Yes, this brings me back.
I started saying that, when I was very little, my father worked in a tannery, and we lived on a little town called Wizna.
When I was about four years old, and that must've been in the early '30s, since I was born in '28, my father bought a tannery on the outskirts of town-- a tannery on [?
Kolonyagas. ?]
It was a long street of maybe two miles on the outskirts, and that played an important part in our surviving as well.
But the people who sold us the tannery were Jewish people who took the money and left for Argentina.
And we would always discuss at home, why are we buying a tannery so that others can use the money to leave Poland?
Why aren't we?
And I really don't know what the answer is.

I have a feeling that our family was just too large, the roots were too deep, and breaking away for a part of it to leave Poland was probably a challenge that my father and mother did not want to take.
They did not want to break up the family.
How do you pick up eight children with spouses and grandchildren and-- and leave aunts and uncles behind, and so many of them, and just go off to unknown lands?
But to answer your question, in the 1930s, we began to-- in fact, we-- my father bought a radio.
And we were one of the first families in town to have a radio.
And we began to hear a lot of news about the rise of Nazism.
And-- but people didn't believe everything they heard.
Jews-- Jewish people-- have been persecuted for thousands of years.
Everywhere we where in many countries we were subjected to antisemitism and to persecution.
This was nothing new.
So there is a mad man by the name of Hitler, who is-- who wrote a book, saying that he would annihilate the Jewish people altogether if he had the chance.

But we-- my parents probably felt, well, that's another Khmelnysky, or another situation like in Spain, or another situation like in many other parts of the world, where we always managed to get by it and to survive and to go on.
And so they probably denied the seriousness of it, and therefore, did nothing to take steps to get out of Poland when we still could do that.
Unfortunately, before too long, it was too late.
Of course, the Polish government was terribly antisemitic as well.
We had a president by the name of Pilsudski and a premier by the name of Moscicki, both of whom with terrible antisemites.
And so the Poles-- the farmers and the peasants-- had blind antisemitism that they really didn't know why, but they knew that the Jews could be used as a scapegoat.
The government did not stop it.
In fact, the government supported it.
And before you know it, they made it impossible for Jews to get out altogether.
So we were locked in.
Well, we heard rumors that war was coming to Europe.
And we heard, of course, about Hitler and the demands he made on Czechoslovakia and then Austria, the Anschluss.
And we heard about Chamberlain and the visit to Munich and the appeasement and all that.
We heard that demands were made on Poland, the Danzig area where many Germans lived.
They wanted that annexed to Germany.

But we thought all of it would somehow be worked out and settled and that certainly France and England were ready and able to defend themselves and the rest of the countries in Europe against any Nazi-- any German designs.
And so we-- we were worried but we were not-- we did not realize the total danger that lay ahead.
In-- on September 1st, 1939, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germany was attacked from both sides.
We then found-- I mean, Poland was attacked from both sides.
We found out that Hitler and Stalin signed a non-aggression pact, which divided Poland into two halves along a long river that divides Poland in half.
And the Germans marched in within a few days.
The Polish army, which consisted of some soldiers riding horses and carrying swords, were certainly no match for the German Blitzkrieg, Panzers, and airplanes, and artillery.
And they were cut to pieces in a matter of days.
And soon after September 1st, the German army marched into Jasionowka.
But to our amazement, they did not burn.
They did not loot.
They do not kill.
They were rather polite.
And they announced that the reason is that they had signed an agreement with Russia and that Jasionowka was going to be part of the Soviet sphere, so they cannot do anything.
They will be pulling out and the Russians will be pulling in.
And this is what happened a day or two later.
Their Russians came in.
The Jewish people were very happy with that.
And my family had some concerns, because the Russians regarded anybody who owned a factory or a tannery as being a member of the-- of the capitalist establishment.
And many of them were deported to Siberia.
In retrospect, it would have been a blessing had we been deported to Siberia.
The Minskis, who owned the largest tannery, were deported and most of them survived and ended up, eventually, in Israel after the war.
We were not deported.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] once the Soviets came in, things changed for the better.
Officially, antisemitism was abolished.
You could have somebody arrested for even a remark, an antisemitic remark.
The government was against antisemitism As a practical matter, there was anti-Semitism among the Russians as well, but not sponsored by the government.
As for myself, my school was changed form a Hebrew school to a Russian school-- school, called a [?
Dziesiecletke, ?] which means a ten year school.
Dziesiec ?] is ten.
Letke ?] is year.
And I suddenly realized that education is important.
And from being a very bad student in the Hebrew school, I became an excellent student in the Russian school.
I learned Russian very quickly.
I had excellent marks.
And as a reward, I was elected to be the president of a pioneer group.
Now the-- in the Soviet system, young children were pioneers.
They wore red scarves and a pin.
From pioneer, when you went into high school, you joined the Komsomol.
And from the Komsomol, if you were a good, obedient member, eventually you joined the Communist Party.
So I was a pioneer, and president of the pioneer.
And I loved it.
And again, life was wonderful for me as a child.
My father was not deported.
We were allowed to work.
We were allowed to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] benefit from some of the technological improvements that the Russians had brought.
And all in all, it was not bad.
Jewish institutions were affected, most of them, actually, for the better.
They did not do away with the Yiddish library.
They discouraged religion against all people.
Religion was the opium of the people, according to communist doctrine.
But it wasn't a strict enforcement.
Jews were still able to pray, still have-- had their institutions.
We were still able to celebrate the holidays of Passover and all the others.
And at the same time, we were given a chance to join some aspects of Soviet society.
And it-- it wasn't terribly oppressive.
Only for those who were very rich or those who had committed crimes, they were strict.
But other than that, it was not bad.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody really knew what the pact between Stalin and Hitler was, or what it meant.
At least for the moment, we were grateful we were under Soviet occupation and not in the war.
However, many people felt that this is too good to be true and it would end, as indeed it did.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was really-- I remember very clearly on a weekend-- it was, I think, a Saturday morning-- my niece, who was three years younger than I, Roske, and I went into the fields to gather flowers and to play.
I think I mentioned that, by that time, we lived on the outskirts of town.
We had a larger house, a tannery.
We had some fields behind the tannery.
We actually had two ponds with fish.
And I remember going swimming there.
And we had woods not far-- in back of the fields, the woods started, which eventually played an important part in our surviving.
And on that Saturday morning-- it was June 21st, 1941-- Roske and I were in the field, gathering flowers, when suddenly we heard planes coming.
And we saw the planes come quite low over the town, and we heard explosions.
Bombs were falling.

We ran home to find out that Germany attacked the Soviet Union-- without warning, of course-- and started the war against the Soviet Union, and the bombs were being dropped on the Minski factory, which was an important leather factory, producing leather for the Soviet army.
And so that was when the horror of the Holocaust began, really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, it took the Germans-- again, with Blitzkrieg-- only a few days to demolish the Russian army.
The Russian army was certainly better equipped than the Polish army, but they were no match for the Germans.
They were disorganized.
Thousands and thousands were taken captive.
The Germans were using paratroopers.
They would drop paratroopers in back of the front, surround a whole army, and liquidate them or capture them.
And before you know it, we could see Russian soldiers running around in total disarray, in total confusion, not knowing what was going on.
They kept assuring us that, oh, we'll-- we'll fight the Germans.
We'll drive them out.
But we knew that things were bad.
And it was just one week later after hostilities began, again on the weekend, that the Germans marched into Jasionowka.
And this time, they were not so nice.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The night that they marched into Jasionowka, we heard rumors, even before, that wherever they marched in, they burned, they killed, they raped, and they did horrible things to the Jewish people.
So as we knew that they were coming in, my family, living on the outskirts of town, we ran to the woods to hide.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those who did not run, the Germans, with the help of the Polish people-- our neighbors-- rounded up on that day.
They drove everybody to the center of town and lined them up against the church wall.
They set up machine guns ready to machine gun everybody.
They stood there for hours lined up in the center of town, when, evidently-- I was not there, but evidently-- a high German officer drove by.
And he said-- he made a little speech to the Jewish people.
And he said, we could shoot you all right now, but that would be an easy death.
We don't want you to die so easily.
We want you to slave for us.
We want you to work for us.
We want to take every penny you've got, every ounce of strength.
And we will shoot you when we are good and ready.
We will kill you.
In the meantime, you can go back to your towns.
But as they marched in, our Polish neighbors went with them and pointed out most of the houses in the center of town.
And they used flame throwers.
They set those houses on fire.
They burned most of the Jewish homes.
And the first night, they killed just about 10% of the Jewish population.
Now, from my brothers and sisters who did not run away because they had children, and from all of our neighbors.
My own family ran to the woods, where we were hiding.
We stayed overnight.
It was a Sunday morning.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a bunch of Polish young men were on their way to church.
And they've also looking for Jews who were hiding.
They found my family in the woods.
They beat us mercilessly.
One of them picked up a chunk of wood from a fence, from a picket, and hit my father over the head so hard, so many times, that he was completely covered with blood.
One eye was knocked out, and they thought he was dead.
And they dragged him and threw him into a creek.
The rest of us, they drove us to town, telling us that the Germans have lined up the Jews, and we'll be all be executed.
And so they drove us so that we would be included in the-- against the wall to be shot.
By the time we got to town, the Jews had already been dismissed by this German officer.
So that we went back to our house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As soon as we released, we immediately ran back to the woods and we found my father still alive in the creek.
We brought him home.
We paid a neighbor, Bolke, who lived across the street from us.
His daughter went to the next town, [?
Kinochien ?], where there was a drug store, to get some medicine.
We nursed him back to life.
He begged us to let him die, but we didn't.
We nursed him back to life.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] since-- since most of the Jewish homes were burned, the Jews had no homes to go to.
So each family that still had a home took in several other families.
I believe my parents took in four other families.
So there were over 30 people living in our house.
And from that day on, the actual Holocaust became a daily reality, and it was sheer hell.
Every few days, the Germans would drive into town, round up, usually, some young men to do a certain job, like snow plowing or snow cleaning.
They would make them work for a day or two.
Then make them dig a hole, line them up, and shoot them.
Anybody who was taken for a day's work or two day's work never got back unless they managed to escape, through some miracle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Polish neighbors were as bad as the Germans.
In some cases, worse.
They formed police that helped the Germans, and they did horrible things.
They would make terrible demands on us.
They would-- they eventually they set up a Commissar, a German who supervised.
And the Jews had to build a new home for the Commissar.
The Jews had to supply him with anything and everything he wanted, fresh coffee every day.
How they got, it didn't matter.
If you don't get it, I line up a dozen people and shoot them.
The demands were terrible.
The looting-- the Polish neighbors took everything practically they could from us.
And this went on day after day, week after week, between 19-- Ju-- June of 1941 and January of 1943.
By the end of '42, we began to hear some horrible rumors about whole towns and cities being liquidated and taken away to gas-- to concentration camps.
Believe it or not, the Israelis dropped some agents in Poland.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister, Feitche-- and I'll talk about her more later-- who kept a diary, describes an agent who came to Jasionowka and told the Judenrat-- every town, they set up a Judenrat, a group of responsible Jews on whom the Germans could make demands.
It so happens that my cousin, and eventually my brother-in-law, [?
Schlamisch ?], who's the-- was the head of the Judenrat.

And this Israeli agent, a partisan who came-- who was dropped into Poland, told us about-- he told the Judenrat about what's happening, that Jews are being liquidated by the hundreds of thousands in concentration camps.
Some of them going to labor camps, and others go into a gas chambers, where they are being gassed and burned in crematorium.
Thus, we knew that there was very little hope.
On the other hand-- that was the bad news.
The good news was that, by the end of '42, we began to hear that-- we began to hear that things were not going so well on the fronts for the Germans.

Certainly, later on, we heard about Stalingrad and the horrible defeat that the Germans suffered at Stalingrad where some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whole army was surrounded and destroyed and cap-- captured by the Russians.

So suddenly, there was a ray of hope, that if you could only, somehow, provide for hiding places, or make some arrangements, or survive maybe another year or two, maybe the Germans will be defeated, and maybe you'll have a chance to survive.

In order to prepare for that, my father and my older siblings, my brother, Meyer, who was, I don't know, maybe 16 years older than I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my brother, Maishe, who was fours years older than I, they begin to plan hiding places.
First of all, we built a little hiding place above the house where every time they would come to round up a few healthy young Jews, my brothers would run up and hide.
But then we also built a very ingenious hiding place above the tannery.
I have a sketch that I drew of it, which I'm including in memoirs, which I'm writing right now.
And-- and that sketch will show how we built a hiding place above the tannery.
I'll describe it when you're photographing it.
And there, we could hide about maybe 15 people for a-- on a temporary basis.
We figured, when liquidation comes, maybe we can run and hide there for a while until such time as we can leave Jasionowka altogether.
In addition, my father and my family negotiated with a farmer who lived in the forest.
And this farmer said to us, if you'll give me all of your money and gold and leather and whatever you've got, I will give you shelter when liquidation comes.
I will hide you out if you come to me.
I don't recall his name.
I have it written someplace.
And so we gave him everything we had, just about.
And that's all we could do to prepare for the eventual liquidation date.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After you change the tape, I will start describing the liquidation itself, which takes a major part of the Holocaust story.
